design_philosophy.core_loop: Destroy enemies to earn currency → Purchase weapons/aircraft upgrades → Progress through levels with customized loadout → Repeat with new tactical choices
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management between offensive power, defensive capability, and fuel/durability constraints; balancing immediate survival against future purchasing power
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Mercenary pilot commanding advanced fighter jets in covert military operations, with autonomy to customize equipment loadout before each mission
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Energy bar system instead of lives-based progression creates persistent damage consequences; shop-based weapon economy uniquely ties destruction directly to upgrading capability; side-scrolling format contrasts with Capcom's own vertical shooter tradition, deliberately positioning it as accessible alternative; pilot selection provides meaningful mechanical differentiation rare in arcade shooters

difficulty.lives_system: Single energy bar that depletes with damage; one bar per life, multiple lives per continue
difficulty.continues_system: Continues available; progression through shop purchases carries forward across attempts
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression with shop access between levels; no mid-level checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: pilot selection (3 planes with different capabilities), aircraft variety (multiple craft per pilot in SNES), weapon selection before entering levels, ability to convert unused weapons to currency, choice of which enemies to prioritize for income

progression.upgrade_system: Economic: earn money by destroying enemies; purchase aircraft, special weapons, and defenses in inter-level shop; unused weapons convert back to currency to avoid waste penalty
progression.unlock_mechanism: Aircraft and weapon availability determined by accumulated currency; starting with $3000 and basic F8 Crusader
replayability_hooks: Three distinct pilot characters with different plane/stat profiles encourage multiple playthroughs
Multiple aircraft per pilot in SNES version creates different loadout strategies
Weapon variety allows experimentation with different tactical approaches to same levels
Economic optimization: discovering efficient enemy-destruction patterns to maximize purchasing power

world_building_techniques: Pilot character selection screen establishes mercenary identity
Manga source material (Area 88) provides strategic context in Japanese version
North American version strips manga references, emphasizing military framing over character narrative
Mission-based level structure implies ongoing campaign against Project 4 terrorist organization

faction_indicators: Project 4 as antagonist faction (implied terrorist organization)
Military/UN framing in North American localization
danger_signals: Mercenary employment context suggests morally ambiguous protagonist
Military action framework with unnamed combatants
environmental_storytelling: Aircraft selection and customization reflects pilot competence and operational resources
Level progression through different combat zones implies expanding theater of operations
Enemy variety (planes and vehicles) establishes military infrastructure targets

sources_used: wikipedia - U.N. Squadron gameplay section, development notes, release information, reception data
